{"success": true, "name": "client-feedback-pipeline", "description": "Process, prioritize, and implement client feedback on a live website. Covers feedback capture, analysis, change implementation across locales, ethical content decisions (removing fabricated data), and roadmap updates.", "tags": [], "related_skills": [], "content": "--- name: client-feedback-pipeline description: Process, prioritize, and implement client feedback on a live website. Covers feedback capture, analysis, change implementation across locales, ethical content decisions (removing fabricated data), and roadmap updates. triggers: - "client feedback" - "process feedback" - "implement changes from client" - "website revisions" - "client requested changes" - "update site from feedback" - "remove fake data" - "ethical content audit" --- # Client Feedback Pipeline Goal: Take raw client feedback (WhatsApp voice notes, texts, emails) and turn it into a clean, prioritized set of changes applied across all locales. ## Phase 1: Capture Save the raw feedback verbatim. The client's exact words matter for: - Distinguishing what they actually said vs what you inferred - Understanding their emotional framing ("that's bullshit", "I don't want to put something false") - Preserving approval-ready copy they already wrote **Save to a file** in the project under `docs/client-feedback-YYYY-MM-DD.md`. When the client writes section-by-section copy (e.g. "Our Services: The section is now clean..."), save that too — it's approved text you'll use directly. ## Phase 2: Analyze — Sort into 4 Buckets | Bucket | Signal | Action | |--------|--------|--------| | **Delete** | "remove", "quit", "doesn't need to be there", "unnecessary" | Remove the section/content immediately | | **Simplify** | "too much", "overcomplicated", "unclear" | Reduce to minimum viable version | | **Rewrite** | "I want it like this", client-provided copy | Replace with client's exact words | | **Ethical red flag** | "that's bullshit", "not real", "false", "fabricated" | Remove immediately, no negotiation | **Key insight:** When a client says fabricated data is "bullshit", don't argue or replace it with different data. Remove it. An empty section is honest; a fake one is a liability. ## Phase 3: Prioritize 1. **Ethical red flags** — first, always. These are legal/trust issues. 2. **Navigation/structure** — affects every page view 3. **Homepage hero** — highest visibility 4. **Content sections** — body of pages 5. **About page** — brand story 6. **Footer/SEO/meta** — lingering details ## Phase 4: Implement (Multi-Locale) ### JSON Field Verification: Data Added ≠ Data Rendered After adding data to JSON, **always verify the component actually renders it via a browser check.** Adding a new key like `resultsParagraphs` to the content JSON does NOT mean the page will display it — the React component must explicitly read and render that field. **Workflow for new-content rendering verification:** 1. Add data to all 4 locale JSON files 2. Update the component (source file in node_modules or monorepo) to read and render the new field 3. If the component is from a monorepo package: update dist/ AND copy the change upstream so it persists across npm install 4. Full clean build (rm -rf .next + docker build --no-cache) 5. Deploy with --force 6. Navigate to the page in a browser and visually confirm the content appears 7. If it doesn't appear: check that the component doesn't have an early return that skips the new content (`if (!d.title && !paragraphs.length) return null` may skip rendering even though other fields exist) **Do NOT rely on curl or grep to verify content rendering when PPR is active** — use browser screenshots or the snapshot tool. ### Turbopack src/ Resolution Trap (Monorepo Packages) **Turbopack may resolve `@scope/sections` from `packages/@scope/sections/src/` rather than `dist/`, even when `package.json` exports point to `./dist/index.js`.** This means: copying fresh dist to `node_modules/@scope/sections/dist/` is **not sufficient** when the site has a `packages/@scope/sections/` directory in the build context. Turbopack finds the source files first. **The fix for Docker builds with monorepo `file:` dependencies:** ```bash # Copy BOTH src and dist from the monorepo to the Docker context cp -r ../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections/src/* packages/@ai-whisperers/sections/src/ cp -r ../ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections/dist/* packages/@ai-whisperers/sections/dist/ ``` The `packages/@ai-whisperers/` directory in the site repo is the Docker build context for `COPY . .` — Turbopack resolves from the `src/` subdirectory during `next build`. If only `dist/` is fresh but `src/` is stale, Turbopack compiles the stale source. **Diagnosis:** Check the builder-stage container: ```bash docker run --rm --target=builder grep 'newFieldName' /app/packages/@ai-whisperers/sections/src/component.tsx ``` If the new field is in `node_modules/` dist but NOT in `packages/@ai-whisperers/sections/src/`, the output SSG won't have it. ### Edge Cache Bypass for Verification Cloudflare edge cache can serve stale HTML for hours after deployment. Three bypass methods: **1. Direct container check (when you have Docker access):** ```bash docker exec $(docker ps -q -f name=) grep 'expected content' /app/.next/server/app/en/page.html ``` **2. `--resolve` bypass (bypasses DNS → Traefik directly):** ```bash curl -s --resolve "site.com:443:127.0.0.1" -H "Host: site.com" "https://site.com/en/page" ``` **3. Cache-busting headers (less reliable with aggressive CDN):** ```bash curl -sH "Cache-Control: no-cache" -H "Pragma: no-cache" "https://site.com/en/page" ``` **Note:** React renders text nodes with `` comment separators. `◷09:00` may render as `◷ 09:00` — simple `grep -oP '◷[^<]*'` may miss the text because of the intervening comment. Use `grep -oP '◷.*?09:00'` or check the full `

` element. ### Monorepo Package Components: Changes Must Go Upstream When the content you're adding comes from a component defined in a shared monorepo package (e.g. `@ai-whisperers/sections`), adding data to the JSON is only half the work. The component must also be updated to render the new field. **Workflow for adding a new rendered field to a monorepo-powered site:** 1. **Check where the component lives** — if it's imported from `@scope/sections`, the source is in the monorepo, not in the project's own source tree 2. **Find the source file** — navigate to `node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections/src/.tsx` (the `src/` is what Next.js compiles via `transpilePackages`) 3. **Edit the component** to read the new field from its `data` or `pageContent` prop 4. **Update the `dist/`** — if the component is consumed via `dist/` (look for `exports` in package.json), run the monorepo build or copy the modified source to dist 5. **Commit changes upstream** — the same source file lives in both `node_modules/` (symlinked) and the monorepo root (e.g. `/root/ai-whisperers-base/packages/sections/src/`). After testing, copy the change to the monorepo and commit it so it persists across `npm install` 6. **Force a full clean build** — `rm -rf .next && docker build --no-cache ...` because SSG snapshots may not pick up component changes on partial rebuilds 7. **Verify via browser** — navigate to the page and check the DOM for the new text **Diagnosis: "Data is in JSON but page doesn't show it"** - Check `__NEXT_DATA__` in page source — does `content: {}` or `content: {...}`? If empty, JSON failed to load - Check `dist/.js` for the new field name — if it's not in the compiled output, the `dist/` wasn't rebuilt - Check if the component returns early: `if (!d.title && !paragraphs.length) return null` — if the component only checks `title` and `paragraphs` but the new field lives under a different name, the data IS loaded but never reaches a rendering code path - Place the new section data INSIDE a known-rendered field structure when possible, or extend the component to add a new rendering branch Use `execute_code` with Python's `json` module for large JSON files: **CRITICAL: JSON field additions may not render.** Adding a new key like `resultsParagraphs` to the content JSON does NOT mean the page will display it. The page's React component must explicitly read and render that field. Before adding novel data that doesn't have an existing structural precedent in the file, check whether the page layout component actually consumes it — otherwise the data sits in the JSON unseen. To verify whether a JSON field renders on the page: 1. Deploy the changes (build + deploy) 2. Navigate to the page in a browser 3. Check the DOM for the expected text strings 4. If the text doesn't appear, the component doesn't render that key — either extend the component or restructure the data to use a known-rendered field ```python import json locales = ['es', 'en', 'nl', 'de'] for loc in locales: path = f'/root/nexa-paraguay/content/{loc}.json' with open(path) as f: data = json.load(f) # Check structure (en.json is flat, others are nested) if 'home' in data: home = data['home'] # ... make changes ... else: home = data # flat structure with open(path, 'w') as f: json.dump(data, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False) ``` **Critical:** Verify every locale file after changes: ```bash for f in content/*.json; do python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('$f'))" && echo "$f: OK"; done ``` ## Key Session Insight: Family vs Role Mapping When the founder is married to a Dutch national and the "client" WhatsApp contact is their daughter, **don't assume the daughter is the founder**. Common pattern in LATAM family businesses: | Role | Person | |------|--------| | Founder / CEO | Mother — lived abroad, has the experience, makes decisions | | Content & Tech contact | Daughter — passes feedback, handles day-to-day | | Technical partner | Son/daughter-in-law — builds the site | **The founder's own words are the source of truth for the business.** A daughter's interpretation is second-hand. Always ask: "who said this — the founder directly, or someone relaying?" When the founder sends WhatsApp audios, transcribe them verbatim and use them as the definitive positioning. Example: "acompañamiento de cerca" (close accompaniment) emerged from one audio and became the core tagline. ## Phase 5: PPR Content Verification 1. `rm -rf .next && npx next build` — must pass with all routes intact 2. Check route count didn't drop unexpectedly 3. Check PPR status on all major routes 4. Commit with a structured message listing what changed per category ## Phase 6: Update Roadmap + Client Analysis After implementing, update: - The upgrade tracker (`upgrade-tracker.json`) — change Todo→Done for completed items - The complete roadmap (`docs/13-upgrades/complete-roadmap.md`) — mark statuses - The client analysis (`docs/client-analysis-complete.md`) — refresh with new understanding ## Phase 7: Screenshot Baseline After all changes are deployed, create a screenshot baseline of every live page to document the current state: 1. **Identify all page slugs** from `nexa-pages/*.json` or equivalent 2. **Identify all blog article slugs** from content/blog/posts-*.json 3. **Capture full-page screenshots** for every slug × every locale using Playwright: ```bash python3 -c " import subprocess, os, json outdir = '/path/to/screenshots/full' os.makedirs(outdir, exist_ok=True) for slug in ['home', 'servicios', 'sobre', ...]: for loc in ['nl', 'en', 'es', 'de']: url = f'https://site.com/{loc}/{slug}' if slug != 'home' else f'https://site.com/{loc}' path = f'{outdir}/{loc}-{slug}.png' subprocess.run(['playwright', 'screenshot', '--viewport-size', '1440,900', '--full-page', url, path], timeout=60) " ``` 4. **Remove `screenshots/` from `.gitignore`** so the baseline is versioned 5. **Commit** with a message listing the scope (how many pages × locales) 6. **Later screenshots** can be compared against this baseline to show progress **Screenshot naming convention:** `{locale}-{slug}.png` — lowercase, no trailing slash. Blog articles: `{locale}-blog-{slug[:45]}.png`. ## Phase 1b: Pricing Model Discovery When market research assumptions about pricing turn out wrong after the client meeting, follow this pattern: ### The Problem A common pattern in professional-services client work: 1. You do market research → find competitors at $2,900-$6,900 2. You assume similar pricing and build everything around those tiers 3. Client meeting confirms price is **$1,500** — completely different The risk is that old assumptions linger in docs and mislead everyone. ### The Fix: Deprecation + Changelog + Restructure #### 1. Create a CHANGELOG.md Document every assumption that was invalidated. Table format: | Old Assumption | What You Had | New Reality | Source | |----------------|-------------|-------------|--------| | Core price | $2,900 | **$1,500** | Meeting §1.1 | | Revenue model | Fees = main income | **Commissions** = real money | Meeting §1.3 | | Costs/mo | $10K-$18K | **~$0 fixed** | Meeting §1.4 | Add a `CHANGELOG.md` to `docs/` with full before/after for every changed assumption. #### 2. Add Deprecation Banners For every doc that was built on wrong assumptions, add a banner at the top: ```markdown > **⚠️⚠️⚠️ DEPRECATED — NOT CURRENT ⚠️⚠️⚠️** > This document was built on the $X assumptions that were **invalidated** by the YYYY-MM-DD meeting. > See `docs/meetings/meeting-report-YYYY-MM-DD.md` and `docs/CHANGELOG.md` for current data. > Keep this file as historical reference only. DO NOT use for business decisions. ``` Files that typically need banners: - `pricing-matrix-complete.md` (if pricing changed) - `financial-model.md` (if revenue/cost projections changed) - `site-audit.md` (if client rejected recommendations) #### 3. Rethink the Pricing Model on Site The pattern that works for professional-services websites: ``` 1 base price (transparent, fixed) + day rates + commission-funded (free to client) + custom quotes ``` Not 4 program tiers. The client's real revenue model is different from what consultants assume. #### 4. Update Cross-References Point README.md, SOURCE_OF_TRUTH.md, and document hierarchy trees to the new meeting report, not the old pricing docs. --- ## Service Matrix Walkthrough (210-Service Questionnaire) When you have a comprehensive service inventory (200+ services across phases, from pre-arrival to premium) and need the client to mark which they do/don't do and what they're interested in: ### Format Build a walkable document organized **chronologically by client journey phase**, not by service category importance: | Phase | Categories | Services | Meeting Time | |-------|-----------|----------|-------------| | 0 — Pre-Arrival | Documents, Consulting, Logistics | 40 | ~11 min | | 1 — Arrival & First Week | Airport, ID, Banking, Rentals, Purchase | 60 | ~16 min | | 2 — Weeks 2-12 | Family, Health, Transport, Home, Business | 56 | ~17 min | | 3 — Months 3-12 | Tax, Residency, Lifestyle, Premium | 54 | ~12 min | | **Total** | **17 categories** | **210** | **~56 min** | ### Each Service Entry ```markdown | # | Service | ¿Sonia lo hace? | ¿Incluido en $1,500? | Precio si extra | Notas | |---|---------|-----------------|----------------------|----------------|-------| | 5 | Poder notarial (para compras remotas) | ☐ Sí ☐ No | ☐ Sí ☐ No | $________ | 🔴 PREGUNTA CLAVE | ``` **Column meanings:** 1. Service number + name 2. Client checkbox: Sí / No 3. Client checkbox: included in base price or extra 4. Price field if extra 5. Notes / priority flags (🔴 = critical, 🟡 = needs follow-up, 💡 = opportunity) ### Post-Meeting Output Three lists produced from the walkthrough: 1. **✅ Confirmed** — add to site content and pricing pages 2. **💡 Investigate** — topics the client found interesting but needs to think about. Add to task tracker with assignee. 3. **❌ Rejected** — documented with reason so the team never asks again ### Summary Table After the meeting, fill a summary: | Category | Total | ✅ Sí | ❌ No | 💡 Investigar | |----------|-------|-------|-------|--------------| | Documentos | 20 | _ | _ | _ | | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | | **TOTAL** | **210** | **_** | **_** | **_** | ### Pitfalls - Do NOT send this cold via WhatsApp — it's a MEETING document - Do NOT read every service name aloud — let the client scan visually for categories they care about - Do mark everything during the meeting — memory is unreliable - Do NOT treat "I don't know" as "no" — mark as 💡 and move on - The client will naturally spend more time on Phase 1 (arrival) than Phase 3 (premium). That's fine. - Flag services marked ❓ in the original matrix (like "tax situation analysis", "flight booking assistance") — these are the ones we truly don't know. The others are confirmations of assumptions. - Keep a timer per phase. 56 min is the limit — if you go over, skip Phase 3 (it's least critical). ### Reference Full 210-service questionnaire template for Nexa Paraguay at `docs/meetings/service-matrix-questions-sonia.md`. --- ## Competitive Analysis Deep-Dive (Against a Specific Competitor Site) When the client mentions a specific competitor or you discover one during research, and you need to understand everything about them to find differentiation opportunities: ### Process 1. **Full site crawl** — Navigate to every top-level page (home, services, pricing, about, FAQ, blog, contact, 404) 2. **Extract pricing** — what they charge, for what services, with what payment methods 3. **Identify team** — who runs it, their background, their public persona 4. **Analyze positioning** — what ideology/audience they target, what fear/hope they trigger 5. **Compare point-by-point** — build a comparison table across every dimension ### Comparison Table Template ```markdown | | Competitor | Our Client | |---|---|---| | Price | €X (product) | **$Y** | | Positioning | Freiheit/Plan B/Escape | Family/peace/relax | | Location | Rural (Hohenau) | Urban (Asunción) | | Team | 4+ people + lawyers | Solo + daughter + network | | Post-residency | Limited | Strong (up to 1 year) | | Blog | Political + tax | Practical + personal | | Bot | Telegram (manual) | WhatsApp (automated) | ``` ### Output: Three Lists #### 1. Steal These Immediately (free, fast, identical adaptation) - FAQ page structure - Email capture on every page - Lead magnets (topic adaptation) - Testimonials slider - Transparent pricing display - 404 page with email capture #### 2. Adapt In Next Sprint (same function, different approach) - Their most popular blog post (legal/technical) → client's version (simple/personal) - Their calculator (residency timing) → client's calculator (tax comparison) - Their landing page (Plan B/crisis) → client's version (new chapter/positive) #### 3. Don't Bother With - Political content (would alienate client's audience) - Paid group expeditions (too complex for client's bandwidth) - YouTube channel (client doesn't want content creator role) - Crypto payments (not needed at client's volume) ### Key Principle The competitor analysis is NOT about copying everything. It's about finding the **differentiation gap** and exploiting it. If the competitor costs €2,800 and the client costs $1,500, that's not a weakness — it's the headline. Save the full analysis as a reference file under the relevant skill: ```markdown `references/competitor-analysis-{competitor-name}.md` Structure: who they are, pricing, site structure, content strategy, what to steal, what to avoid, what our client has that they don't ``` --- ## Phase 1e: Client Discovery Meeting Prep (Spanish-Language Client) When you need to go through questions with a Spanish-speaking client in a structured meeting after research, the `meeting-prep` skill covers internal calendar briefs. For **client-facing meetings**, use this format. ### Structure a Walkable Document Organize questions into blocks ordered for conversation flow, not by topic importance. The first block should always be "show what we built" so the client sees progress. | Block | Topic | Questions | Time | |-------|-------|-----------|------| | 0 | Check-in + show & tell | 1 | 5 min | | 1 | **Pricing** (CRITICAL) | 8 | 12 min | | 2 | Services scope | 6 | 10 min | | 3 | Site content + voice | 6 | 10 min | | 4 | Photos & assets | 4 | 8 min | | 5 | WhatsApp bot + ops | 5 | 8 min | | 6 | Competition | 4 | 5 min | | 7 | Close + actions | — | 5 min | | **Total** | | **34-40** | **~60 min** | ### Each Question Format Every question needs: 1. **The exact wording** in the client's language (Spanish for Paraguayan clients). Not English with a translation thought — the actual Spanish question they'll hear. 2. **Context** — why we need it (1-2 sentences hidden from client, for our reference) 3. **Expected answer** — what they'll probably say (helps you prepare counter-questions) 4. **Action per answer** — what we do for each possible response. If answer X → do Y. If answer Z → do W. 5. **Notes field** — blank space to fill during the call ### Appendices - **Appendix A: Already Answered** — questions we DON'T ask again. List each with source meeting and confirmed answer. This prevents re-litigating settled topics. - **Appendix B: Error Table** — old assumptions vs new reality. Show this at the beginning when pricing discussion comes up so the client sees we understand the delta. ### Phase Communication Strategy Don't send all questions at once. Clients (especially small business owners) get overwhelmed. | Phase | What | Channel | |-------|------|---------| | **Urgent** (during meeting) | 3-4 pricing questions + photo request | In person / video call | | **This week** | Service definitions in a markable doc | Google Doc + WhatsApp | | **Build-time** | Operational questions per task | WhatsApp per item | ### Pitfalls - Don't use English wording with Spanish-speaking clients and expect them to follow. Write the question IN Spanish. - Don't re-ask settled questions. Appendix A exists for this reason — check it before the meeting. - If a client's family member relays information but the founder is the decision maker, the relayed info is NOT the source of truth. Always validate with the founder. - Build-time questions should be asked INDIVIDUALLY, not batched. The client will ignore a list of 10 WhatsApp messages but will answer a single question. - The "notes field" is critical. You will NOT remember what they said 30 minutes into the conversation. ## Phase 1f: Docs Restructure After Major Client Feedback ## Phase 1d: Docs Restructure After Major Client Feedback When new client info invalidates old documents, follow this process: ### 1. Audit the Damage Find every file that references the now-wrong assumptions: - Search for wrong prices, wrong tiers, wrong features - Check cross-references between docs - Note stale dates ### 2. Restructure the Directory Clean up loose root-level files: - Move standalone files into numbered subdirectories (01-client/, 09-market-intelligence/, etc.) - Move HTML deliverables to `docs/deliverables/` - Create `docs/meetings/` for meeting records - Move raw transcripts to `docs/meetings/` ### 3. Fix the Navigation Update `docs/README.md`: - Fix any wrong paths (e.g., repo paths that changed) - Point to the meeting report as the key document, not outdated pricing docs - Add status columns (✅ Current, ⚠️ Deprecated, 🟡 Needs Update) to file tables - Add a "Start Here" section pointing to CHANGELOG.md ### 4. Update SOURCE_OF_TRUTH.md - Add a note at the top: "For meeting outcome, see docs/meetings/meeting-report-YYYY-MM-DD.md" - Replace any "UPCOMING" sections with "OUTCOME" sections - Update document hierarchy tree - Update integrity check / status tables ### 5. Commit Message Format ``` docs: full restructuring post-YYYY-MM-DD - Created CHANGELOG.md — every assumption that changed - Added deprecation banners to pricing-matrix-complete.md, financial-model.md - Moved N root-level files into proper subdirectories - Moved N HTML deliverables to docs/deliverables/ - Fixed README.md wrong paths + cross-references - Updated SOURCE_OF_TRUTH.md with outcome sections ``` --- ## Pitfalls ## Founder-Direct vs Relayed Feedback Not all client feedback is equal. Two distinct patterns: ### Pattern A: Relayed Feedback (Assistant relays founder's words) - **Source:** Luana, Kiki, Ivan — someone who ISN'T the founder - **Signal:** "she says", "my mom wants", "Sonia said to remove" - **Risk:** Interpretation drift — the relay may soften, amplify, or filter what the founder actually wants - **Handling:** Implement what's asked, but flag any claim that seems to conflict with the founder's known values. Save the relayed message verbatim. - **Example:** Luana said "remove intake wizard" — relayed clearly, no interpretation. Implement immediately. ### Pattern B: Founder-Direct Feedback (Founder streams raw content) - **Source:** Sonia herself — WhatsApp text, voice notes, or in-person - **Signal:** First-person narrative ("I lived 7 years in the Netherlands"), raw unstructured stream of consciousness - **Risk:** The content is gold but unstructured — needs extraction into a gap matrix - **Handling:** 1. Save the **verbatim message** to a reference file 2. **Do NOT** dump it directly into the site content — it's raw, it needs structuring 3. Extract specific data points: numbers ("10 personas"), services ("Airbnb temporal, chip local, colegios"), process steps 4. Cross-reference against the live site's content JSON files to build a gap matrix 5. The gap matrix shows what's already implemented vs what's missing 6. Propose structured content changes for approval before implementing ### The Gap Matrix Process (Founder-Direct Only) When a founder sends free-form narrative content: 1. **Read all current locale content files** (es/en/nl/de content/*.json) 2. **Build a matrix** with columns: `Feature | Has Content? | Where | Missing?` 3. For each distinct point in the founder's message: - Is it already in the site content? If yes, mark ✅ and note where - Is it partially covered? 🟡 - Is it completely absent? 🔴 — this is a gap 4. Present the matrix to the user before implementing 5. Only implement gaps after the user confirms **Example matrix output:** ``` | Feature | Status | Notes | |---|---|---| | 7 años en Holanda | 🔴 No está | Sobre Nexa dice genérico | | Música → mamá | 🔴 No está | Historia personal faltante | | 10 personas ayudadas | 🔴 No está | Primer stat real disponible | | Airbnb temporal/chip local | 🔴 No está | Proceso incompleto | | Colegios/seguro médico | 🔴 No está | No mencionado en servicios | | Contaduría network confiable | 🔴 No está | Honestidad = valor | | Requisitos de residencia (lista) | ✅ NL/EN | ES/DE faltan | ``` ## Multi-Tenant Path Cleanup (Post-Migration) After migrating a site from a multi-tenant builder (path pattern `/s/{locale}/{site}/...`) to standalone (`/{locale}/...`), EVERY content file may still contain broken multi-tenant paths in: - Footer column links - Header navigation CTAs - Hero section CTAs - Section-level CTA hrefs - Blog content (MDX/JSON inline links) - `ctaHref` on booking forms - `linkHref` on privacy/legal notices - Any `"/s/{locale}/{site}/..."` string in JSON **Verification grep:** ```bash grep -rn "/s/[a-z]/" content/*.json grep -rn '"/servicios"' content/*.json # missing locale prefix ``` **Fix scope:** Search ALL 4 locale files. The locale code in the path varies (`/s/nl/...`, `/s/en/...`, `/s/es/...`, `/s/de/...`). Replace with the corresponding `/{locale}/` prefix. ## Page Removal (Cross-Cutting) When a client says "remove page X", it's not just deleting a file. The page is referenced across the entire site structure: | Concern | Where to fix | What to change | |---------|--------------|----------------| | Route registration | `nexa-pages/{slug}.json` | Delete the file — Next.js won't generate the route | | Sitemap | `src/app/sitemap.ts` or similar | Remove from any fallback/static arrays | | Nav | `site.json` navigation array | Remove the nav entry | | Nav (per-locale) | `content/*.json` navigation items | Remove the nav entry in ALL 4 files | | Footer links | `content/*.json` footer columns | Remove in ALL 4 files | | Internal CTA references | `content/*.json` section CTAs | Replace `href: "/programas"` with a valid alternative page | | Types | `src/types.ts` | Remove `{slug}Page?: PageSectionContent` from SiteContent | | Booking forms | `src/components/BookingFormSection.tsx` | Update "back to X" links | | Page content blocks | `content/*.json` | Remove the entire `{slug}Page` block if it exists | | Empty placeholder strings | `content/*.json` (en/nl specifically) | Remove 70+ `"{slug}Page": ""` entries from nested section objects | **Verification:** After removal, grep for the slug name across the entire repo — any remaining reference is a broken link waiting to happen. ## Locale Leak Detection Spanish text in NL and EN locale files is a common artifact of template-based content generation. Check specifically: - **"En resumen"** heading — appears in `highlights.title` under `whyCountryPage` or similar sections - **FAQ questions in Spanish** — `"¿Cuántos viajes..."`, `"¿Cuánto tiempo..."` - **Highlight card titles** — `"8–12 semanas"`, `"Un solo interlocutor"`, `"Precio fijo"` - **Highlight descriptions** — `"Proceso completo desde presentación."`, `"Nexa coordina todos los profesionales."` **Fix:** Translate each to the target locale. For EN: `"8–12 weeks"`, `"Single point of contact"`, `"Fixed price"`. For NL: `"8–12 weken"`, `"Eén aanspreekpunt"`, `"Vaste prijs"`. **Search pattern across all locales:** ```bash grep -rn '"¿' content/nl.json content/en.json content/de.json # Spanish question marks grep -rn '"En resumen"' content/ # Spanish summary heading ``` - **Client action blockers (photo, QR scan, DNS).** Three common items that I CANNOT implement because they require client-side action: 1. **Photos** — Hero images, team portraits, location shots. The site uses AI placeholders. Nothing to replace until the client sends real photos. 2. **WhatsApp bot QR** — The bot is configured and running. The QR must be scanned from the client's WhatsApp app (Settings → Linked Devices). This is a 30-second physical action that unlocks lead qualification. 3. **Domain DNS** — The server (Traefik) is configured to listen for the custom domain. The client must create an A record at their registrar pointing to the VPS IP. Document all three with clear next-step instructions when the session ends. They are NOT technical blockers. - **PPR static shell caching** — When PPR (`cacheComponents: true`) is active, the static HTML shell for `[locale]` is frozen at build time. Changing `nexa-pages/home.json` does NOT update the visible content until the Docker image is rebuilt AND `--force` deployed. `docker service update --image ...` with the same tag may skip the restart. Always use `docker service update --force --image ...`. - **SectionRenderer content key mismatch** — Components like `HeroSection` do `pageContent.hero` assuming `pageContent` is the `home` sub-object. If the section config has `"content": "home.hero"`, the resolved data is `home.hero` but the component looks for `pageContent.hero` which is `home.hero.hero` = null. Change content key to `"home"` so `pageContent = content.home` and `pageContent.hero` resolves correctly. For sections that use `data` directly (FeedbackSection), use the deep path `"home.feedback"`. # Professional-Services Website (Architecture A)

Build a standalone Next.js 15 site for a professional services firm
(consultancy, law firm, advisory, B2B supplier) with dual-audience routing
or single-audience lead gen.

**Two distinct sub-types:**

| Sub-type | Example | Content Pattern | CTA Pattern |
|----------|---------|----------------|-------------|
| **B2C Advisory** | Golden Visa, Law Firm | Dual-path (investor/business), testimonials, comparison tables | Book a call, contact form |
| **B2B Lead Gen** | Glass supplier, industrial products | Technical specs, project portfolio, certifications | "Request quote" → WhatsApp, quote form |

## When to Use This vs the Builder

| Criteria | Use Standalone (Arch A) | Use Builder (Arch B) |
|---|---|---|
| Dual-path routing (investor vs business) | ✅ | ❌ (single flow) |
| Age-gate / path-choice modal | ✅ | ❌ |
| Complex comparison tables | ✅ | Limited |
| FAQ with multi-tab switching | ✅ | Limited |
| 5+ custom pages | ✅ | ❌ (builder does 1 page) |
| Team has 3+ staff profiles | ✅ | ✅ |
| Simple brochure (home + contact) | ❌ | ✅ |
| B2B with technical product catalog | ✅ | ❌ |
| Quote request form | ✅ | ❌ |
| No prices (custom quoting) | ✅ N/A | ✅ N/A |

---

## Sub-type A: B2C Advisory (Golden Visa Pattern)

See the original implementation in `references/golden-visa-mvp-pattern.md`.

Key traits:
- Entry modal (language + path selection)
- Investor and Business landing paths
- Testimonials, trust signals, team profiles
- Dark theme with gold accents

---

## Sub-type E (NEW — May 2026): Food-Service / Meal-Prep Landing Page from Docs Repo

Build a single-page landing page for a food-delivery / meal-prep / subscription food service business, where the client already has a business documentation repo (pricing, copy, brand, ops docs) but no website.

**Reference session:** May 13, 2026 — De Abasto a Casa from meal-prerp-website repo.

### When to Use

- Client has a complete business docs repo (50+ markdown files: pricing, kitchen manuals, brand guidelines, copy, business model)
- Service has 3+ tiered pricing levels with sub-options (individual/pair/family)
- Lead capture is WhatsApp-only (no e-commerce, no cart, no signup)
- Target: Paraguayan consumers, prices in Gs., "vos" informal tone
- Honest marketing: "si no te conviene, te lo decimos" approach
- Brand colors: natural/earthy (green, terracotta, crema)

### Workflow: Docs Repo → Website

```
Step 1: Read the docs repo completely
├── Read: Master Plan (business model, pricing, target)
├── Read: Copy ES (all website copy in markdown)
├── Read: Brand Guidelines (palette, fonts, voice rules)
├── Read: Website Plan (section ordering, page config)
├── Read: Image Curation (which photos to use, specifications)
└── Read: Service Matrix (exact pricing per tier)

Step 2: Structure content into content/es.json
├── Copy every section's text exactly (headlines, subheadlines, descriptions)
├── Preserve the "vos" informal Paraguayan tone
├── Structure into home.hero, home.services (with levels.plans[]), home.howItWorks, etc.
├── Set defaults for calculator inputs from actual market data in the docs
└── Leave placeholder values in (whatsapp, email, testimonial authorizations)

Step 3: Clean up inherited scaffold
├── Remove @ai-whisperers/* deps from package.json (NOT used by standalone landing pages)
├── Delete stale pages/ JSON configs OR rename to pages-json-config/ (Next.js 16 detects pages/ as Pages Router)
├── Delete any app/ directory outside src/ (conflicts with App Router)
├── Remove stale admin/api routes, blog posts, contact forms from template
└── Run npm install with clean lockfile

Step 4: Create components in parallel batches
├── Batch 1: Header (sticky, scroll blur), Hero (gradient, CTAs), Services (3-tier matrix)
├── Batch 2: HowItWorks (timeline 4 steps), Calculator (interactive 'use client'), Gallery (9-placeholder grid)
├── Batch 3: Sourcing (4 pillars), Team, Testimonials, FAQ (accordion), Contact, CTA Banner, Footer, WhatsAppFloat
└── Each component receives content through direct import from @/content/es.json

Step 5: Build & verify
├── npm run build (check for 0 errors)
├── Verify Static Generation: "○ (Static) prerendered as static content"
└── Fix any Pages Router conflicts (rename pages/ directory)

Step 6: Docker build & deploy
├── Use node:20-slim (NOT alpine — lightningcss needs glibc)
├── 3-stage Dockerfile: deps → builder → runner (npm install first, npm run build second, standalone third)
├── docker save | bzip2 | ssh | docker load (image transfer)
└── docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml

Step 7: Post-launch cleanup
├── Replace WhatsApp placeholder with real number (in both site.json and content/es.json)
├── Replace testimonial placeholders (get written authorization per person)
├── Upload real gallery photos (9 images, WebP < 200KB each, 4:3 ratio)
├── Replace email placeholder
└── Add real logo SVG when available
```

### Scaffold Cleanup Checklist

When starting from a template that has e-commerce or multi-tenant scaffold:

```bash
# 1. Remove stale @ai-whisperers deps from package.json
# These ship e-commerce/admin/commerce stacks NOT used by landing pages
# If landing page doesn't import any @ai-whisperers/* package, remove them all

# 2. Handle pages/ directory conflict
# Next.js 16 errors: "pages and app directories should be under the same folder"
mv pages pages-json-config   # If they're JSON configs, not Pages Router pages

# 3. Remove stale app/ directory outside src/
rm -rf app/   # If this contains old admin/content/api routes

# 4. Clean install
rm -rf node_modules .next package-lock.json
npm install
```

### Component Architecture

```
src/components/sections/
├── Header.tsx            # 'use client' — sticky, scroll detect, mobile hamburger
├── Hero.tsx              # 'use client' — gradient bg, CTAs, scroll-to sections
├── Services.tsx           # Server — 3-tier pricing matrix with badges
├── HowItWorks.tsx         # Server — 4-step timeline
├── Calculator.tsx         # 'use client' — interactive savings calculator
├── Gallery.tsx            # Server — 3x3 grid, SVG gradient placeholders
├── Sourcing.tsx           # Server — 4-column trust pillars
├── Team.tsx               # Server — founder profile card
├── Testimonials.tsx       # Server — 3 testimonial cards
├── FAQ.tsx                # 'use client' — accordion
├── Contact.tsx            # Server — split layout CTA + info
├── Footer.tsx             # Server — dark bg, nav links, copyright
└── WhatsAppFloat.tsx      # 'use client' — fixed position pulse button
```

### Component Rules

1. **Server by default** — only 'use client' for interactive pieces (Header scroll, Calculator logic, FAQ accordion, WhatsAppFloat animation)
2. **Import content directly** — each component imports from `@/content/es.json`. Server components use default import; client components do the same (works because Next.js treats JSON modules as both server and client-safe)
3. **Tailwind v4 CSS variable syntax** — use `className="bg-[var(--color-mercado)]"` not `className="bg-mercado"` unless the token is registered in `@theme` and the class is generated
4. **Don't pass props from page.tsx to section components** — each section reads its own data. This keeps page.tsx clean and components self-contained. Only the Hero component optionally accepts overrides for flexibility

### Docker Image for node:20-slim (NOT alpine)

```dockerfile
FROM node:20-slim AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json* ./
RUN npm install

FROM node:20-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
ENV NODE_ENV=production NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-slim AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1 PORT=3000
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public
USER nextjs
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

### Pitfalls

- **@ai-whisperers deps in scaffold**: The template pre-ships 15+ packages from `@ai-whisperers/*` registry. If the landing page doesn't import any, remove ALL of them from both `dependencies` and `devDependencies`. They cause `npm install` (and therefore Docker build) to fail because the packages require a GitHub token for the private registry.
- **pages/ directory conflict**: If the repo has a `pages/` directory with JSON configs, Next.js 16 interprets it as Pages Router and errors: "pages and app directories should be under the same folder". Rename to `pages-json-config/` or `config/pages/`.
- **app/ outside src/**: Some templates have both `app/` (outside src, older admin routes) and `src/app/` (App Router). Delete `app/` — it's stale admin content. Check with `find . -maxdepth 1 -name "app" -type d`. The admin and API routes inside will reference @ai-whisperers packages not installed, causing build errors.
- **node:20-alpine + Turbopack**: Alpine's musl libc can't load `lightningcss.linux-x64-musl.node`. Use `node:20-slim` instead. The image size difference is ~50MB, negligible.
- **Standalone prerendered HTML serving**: Next.js 16 standalone output may return `Cannot GET /` even when prerendered `index.html` exists in `.next/server/app/`. This occurs when the standalone server can't locate prerendered assets. The local `next start` works because it uses the full Next.js server; the standalone `server.js` may not. Workaround: verify locally first, and if standalone fails, use `docker exec` to check the container's `.next/server/app/` directory for the `index.html` file.
- **Calculator wants JSON defaults from content**: The interactive calculator needs sensible defaults from the docs repo (gasto supermercado ~350k Gs/mes, valor hora ~30k Gs, etc.). Extract these from the docs' pricing analysis or client interview data, don't invent them.
- **Testimonial section says "ilustrativo"**: Never ship testimonials as real — label them clearly as placeholders until the client provides written authorization per person.
- **Docker Swarm labels must be under deploy.labels**: In `docker-compose.yml` for Swarm mode, Traefik labels MUST be at `services.web.deploy.labels`, NOT `services.web.labels`. Docker Compose standalone reads labels from `services.web.labels`, but Swarm only reads `deploy.labels`. Labels at the wrong level will exist on the container but Traefik won't see them. Verify with: `docker service inspect <site>_web --format '{{.Spec.Labels}}'`.
- **node:20-slim has no wget**: Healthcheck commands using `wget` will silently fail. Fix: `HEALTHCHECK ... CMD node -e "require('http').get('http://localhost:3000/',r=>process.exit(r.statusCode===200?0:1)).on('error',()=>process.exit(1))"`. This also avoids BusyBox IPv6 resolution issues.
- **Image transfer to VPS without registry**: When no Docker registry is available, transfer via pipe: `docker save <image>:prod | bzip2 | ssh <host> "bunzip2 | docker load"`. Then force service update: `docker service update --image <image>:prod --force <site>_web`. Verify: `docker service ps <site>_web --no-trunc`.
- **Cloudflare DNS not configurable from CLI**: The Cloudflare API token in this environment is read-only for zones. All DNS A records must be added through the Cloudflare web dashboard. Zone paragu-ai.com uses nameservers `elliot.ns.cloudflare.com` / `maria.ns.cloudflare.com`. No API-based DNS automation available.

## Sub-type B: B2B Lead Gen (Pitchy Pattern)

Build a B2B site from the `Ai-Whisperers/template-nextjs-client` template.
This template is e-commerce B2C by default — you must transform it.

### Transformation Checklist (Remove B2C, Add B2B)

**REMOVE these from the cloned template:**
- `app/tienda/` — product store with cart
- `app/envio/` — shipping info
- `app/promociones/` — promotions
- `app/producto/[slug]/` — individual product pages (replace with /productos/ catalog)
- `components/search-autocomplete.tsx` — no product search needed
- `components/dark-mode-toggle.tsx` — B2B stays light theme
- `components/payment-methods.tsx` — no payments
- `components/quick-order.tsx` — no quick ordering
- `components/recently-viewed.tsx` — no recently viewed
- `components/product-card.tsx` — replace with B2B product card (no price)
- `components/hero-carousel.tsx` — single hero image for B2B
- `components/promo-carousel.tsx` — no promotions
- `components/bottom-nav.tsx` — not needed
- `lib/wishlist.ts` and `lib/recently-viewed.ts` — not needed
- `lib/hooks/use-wishlist.ts`, `lib/hooks/use-recently-viewed.ts` — remove, update `lib/hooks/index.ts`
- `public/images/products/` and `public/images/categories/` — template placeholder images
- `public/manifest.json`, `public/sw.js` — PWA files from template

**CREATE these new pages:**
- `app/productos/page.tsx` — Product catalog with specs, no prices, "Request quote" per item
- `app/proyectos/page.tsx` — Portfolio gallery
- `app/servicios/page.tsx` — Service listing with process steps
- `app/cotizar/page.tsx` — Quote request form → WhatsApp

### @ai-whisperers Package Version Mapping

The template pins `^0.1.0` but the GitHub Packages registry has newer versions.
When `npm install` fails with `ETARGET` or `401`, check and set exact versions:

### Content Structure for Blog

The template's blog components expect `blog.posts[]` (array under a posts key).
If your content JSON has `blog` as a flat array, you must adapt the components:

```tsx
// In app/blog/page.tsx — adapt data structure
const b = {
  posts: (c.blog || []),
  categories: [...new Set((c.blog || []).map((p: any) => p.category).filter(Boolean))],
  title: "Blog",
  subtitle: ""
}

// In app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx
const post = ((c.blog || []) as any[]).find((p: any) => p.slug === slug)
```

## Sub-type C (NEW — May 2026): Single-Locale App Router (Standalone Pattern)

Build a clean Next.js 16 App Router site for professional services (law firms, notaries, accounting firms, consultancies) with a single Spanish locale, JSON-driven content, and NO @ai-whisperers dependencies.

**Reference repos:** `Ai-Whisperers/escribania-paraguay`, `Ai-Whisperers/estudio-contable-paraguay`

### When to Use

- Professional services (notary, accounting, law, consulting)
- Single locale (Spanish/Paraguay)
- No e-commerce, no user accounts, no database
- Content-driven (all text in JSON, client doesn't need CMS)
- 6-12 static pages, no dynamic routes beyond service/[slug]
- Brand needs own theme (not builder's generic tokens)

### Stack

| Component | Choice |
|-----------|--------|
| Framework | Next.js 16 + TypeScript 5 + React 19 |
| Router | App Router (`app/`) — flat, no locale prefix |
| Styling | Tailwind v4 (`@tailwindcss/postcss`) + CSS custom properties |
| Icons | lucide-react |
| Fonts | Google Fonts via `next/font/google` |
| Content | JSON files in `content/` (single spanish file) |
| Deploy | Docker Swarm + Traefik on VPS |
| Image | `node:20-alpine`, 3-stage Dockerfile, `output: "standalone"` |
| CI/CD | Manual: `npm run build && docker build && docker stack deploy` |

### Content Architecture (3-Layer)

```
es.json            → ALL text, organized by page: home.hero, serviciosPage.hero, etc.
site.json           → Metadata: name, phone, whatsapp config, social links
pages.json          → Section ordering per page: { "home": { "sections": [...] } }
pages/servicios.json → Individual service data: escrituras, poderes, contabilidad, etc.
```

### Component Development Rules

1. **Server by default, 'use client' only for:**
   - Stateful interactions (accordion, mobile menu, form submission)
   - Browser APIs (IntersectionObserver for scroll reveal)
   - Animation timers (whatsapp float pulse)

2. **Hero component pattern — optional CTAs:**
   ```tsx

     subheadline: string
     ctaPrimaryText?: string   // optional — pages without CTAs omit these
     ctaPrimaryHref?: string
     ctaSecondaryText?: string
     ctaSecondaryHref?: string
     variant?: 'dark' | 'light'
   }
   ```
   Conditionally render the CTA buttons only when props are provided.

3. **CSS variable pattern (Tailwind v4 compatible):**
   ```tsx
   className="bg-[var(--color-primary)] text-white"
   // NOT: className="bg-primary text-white"  (Tailwind v3)
   ```

4. **Theme in globals.css:**

### Service Detail Pages (SSG)

Each service gets its own SSG page at `/servicios/[slug]`:

```tsx
// app/servicios/[slug]/page.tsx
import serviciosData from "@/content/pages/servicios.json"

export function generateStaticParams() {
  const servicios = serviciosData as Record<string, any>
  return Object.keys(servicios).map((slug) => ({ slug }))
}

export default async function ServicioDetailPage({ params }: Props) {
  const { slug } = await params
  const service = servicios[slug]
  if (!service) notFound()
  // Render: ServiceDetailHero → ServiceBenefits → ServiceItemsList → ServiceProcess → CtaBanner
}
```

### Development Cadence for New Sites

1. Scaffold from escribania pattern (copy all components + configs)
2. Replace: `globals.css` theme colors, content/es.json entirely
3. Add/remove pages (remove team/values if not needed, add pricing if needed)
4. Remove unused components
5. Update Dockerfile, docker-compose.yml (image name, domain, Traefik labels)
6. `npm install && npm run build` to verify
7. `docker build && docker stack deploy`

### Pitfalls

- **Tailwind v4 class names differ from v3**: `bg-primary` becomes `bg-[var(--color-primary)]` unless the color is registered in `@theme` (which does generate `bg-primary` etc.). But when in doubt, use the CSS variable syntax directly.
- **The Hero component needs optional CTAs**: Not all pages have CTA buttons. Make `ctaPrimaryText` and `ctaPrimaryHref` optional, not required.
- **Service detail can't pass `icon` prop to ServiceItemsList**: The list component has no icon prop. Remove the prop from the calling code.
- **Content JSON uses {{mustache}} placeholders**: The actual site content in es.json has `{{businessName}}`, `{{whatsapp}}`, `{{phone}}`, `{{email}}`, `{{year}}`, `{{whatsappMessage}}` that components must resolve at render time or replace in the JSON before deployment.
- **Docker Swarm requires external network**: All stacks use `networks: { agent-net: { external: true } }`. Verify the network exists: `docker network ls | grep agent-net`.
- **Reserved port 3000**: In Swarm mode, the `ports: ["3000"]` declaration publishes the port only for the Swarm ingress routing mesh, NOT the host directly. Use `docker exec` or curl through the overlay network to verify.
- **content es.json has site metadata at root level**: The root object includes `siteName`, `siteTitle`, `siteDescription` alongside `navigation`, `footer`, `home.*`, etc. AND `site.json` has overlapping `site.name`, `site.businessName`, etc. Components typically read from `es.json` for display content and `site.json` for whatsapp/meta config.

## Sub-type D: Pages Router Standalone (Nexa Paraguay Pattern)

A Pages Router Next.js site deployed as a standalone Docker image, with a
single locale (ES) and content-driven sections. Images are referenced in
`images.json` and resolved at runtime. Used when the client needs a
lighter-weight site than the full App Router architecture.

### Architecture

```
repo-root/
├── src/pages/
│   ├── index.tsx           # homepage — getServerSideProps loads content + images
│   ├── [slug].tsx          # all other pages — SLUG_MAP routes slugs to page configs
│   └── admin/content.tsx   # optional CMS page
├── src/components/
│   ├── sections.tsx        # SECTION_MAP with HeroSection, ProgramsSection, etc.
│   ├── content.ts          # resolveContent(), resolveImage() helpers
│   ├── Header.tsx           # renders logo + nav
│   └── Footer.tsx
├── nexa-pages/              # page config JSON files (home.json, servicios.json, etc.)
├── content/
│   └── es.json              # all site content (single locale)
├── images.json               # image manifest — all image paths resolved through this
├── public/images/            # actual image files (brand/, hero/, programs/, etc.)
├── Dockerfile                 # must include images.json copying at runtime stage
├── docker-compose.yml         # Traefik labels at service-level, NOT deploy: labels
└── package.json               # may use file:/root/ai-whisperers-base/... local deps
```

### Image Resolution Pattern

Content uses `"@src:trust.certificate"` or `"$img": "programs.tierBase"` syntax
referencing keys in `images.json`. A helper resolves these at runtime:

```typescript
// src/components/content.ts
export function resolveImage(images: any, ref: string): string {
  if (!ref || !images) return ''
  if (ref.startsWith('@img:') || ref.startsWith('@src:')) {
    const key = ref.startsWith('@img:') ? ref.replace('@img:', '') : ref.replace('@src:', '')
    const parts = key.split('.')
    let obj = images
    for (const p of parts) {
      if (obj?.[p]) obj = obj[p]
      else return ''
    }
    return obj?.src || obj?.fallbackSrc || ''
  }
  return ref
}
```

### Critical: images.json Must Be in Docker Image

The Dockerfile does NOT copy the entire repo — only specific dirs. You MUST
add `images.json` explicitly:

```dockerfile
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/images.json ./images.json   # ← REQUIRED
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/nexa-pages ./nexa-pages
```

Without this line, `loadJSON(process.cwd(), 'images.json')` returns null at
runtime and every image reference resolves to empty string — no images
render, no error message.

### Passing Images to Section Components  

Both `index.tsx` and `[slug].tsx` must:

1. Load `images.json` in `getServerSideProps`:
```typescript
const images = loadJSON(process.cwd(), 'images.json')?.images || {}
```

2. Pass it to the return object:
```typescript
return { props: { content, pageConfig, images, pageId: slug, timestamp: Date.now() } }
```

3. Pass it to section components AND reconstruct pageContent properly:
```tsx
if (Component) {
  const sectionData = resolveContent(content, section.content || section.id)
  const pc: any = {}
  const keyName = section.content?.split('.').pop() || section.id
  if (section.content || section.id) pc[keyName] = sectionData || content
  return <Component key={key} pageContent={pc} data={sectionData} images={images} />
}
```
**Why this matters**: Old `sections.tsx` components access data via `pageContent.hero`, `pageContent.stats`, `pageContent.trust` — they expect the key name to be the LAST segment of the content key (`home.hero` → `hero`). Without this reconstruction using `.split('.').pop()`, the pageContent would use `section.id` (e.g. `"stats-counter"`) which doesn't match what the component looks for (`"stats"`), and ALL section data would be invisible despite the component rendering. **This must be applied identically in both `index.tsx` and `[slug].tsx` (they are near-duplicates).**



If `package.json` has `"@ai-whisperers/admin": "file:/root/ai-whisperers-base/..."`,
the Docker build fails because the `file:` path doesn't exist inside the container.

**Fix:** Build on the VPS host first (`npm install && npm run build`), then
build Docker from the already-ready `node_modules`. The `COPY . .` in the
Dockerfile picks up the existing `node_modules` directory, and `npm install
--legacy-peer-deps` runs against the already-installed deps. This works because
npm skips re-installation for packages it can't resolve via `file:` when they're
already in `node_modules`.

### SVG Logo Fallback

When the client has no designer-provided SVG logo, create one inline:

```svg

    <linearGradient id="g" x1="0%" y1="0%" x2="100%" y2="100%">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#C9A96E"/>

  <text x="24" y="88" font-family="'Playfair Display', serif" font-size="64" font-weight="900" fill="#1B2A4A" text-anchor="middle">N</text>
  <text x="68" y="52" font-family="'Playfair Display', serif" font-size="28" font-weight="700" fill="#1B2A4A">Nexa</text>

Create three variants:

- `logo-dark.svg` — white text for dark backgrounds (footer)

```json

### Pitfalls Specific to Pages Router Standalone

For a complete deploy checklist covering ALL items in this section plus verification steps, see `references/client-site-launch-checklist.md`.
For the section component building pattern (FAQ, Blog, Team, Privacy, Glossary, etc.), see `references/section-components-pattern.md`.
For theme standardisation and design-token extraction, see the `src/theme.ts` pattern described below.
For a comprehensive code-quality audit checklist covering the 40+ issues found in a production Pages Router site, see `references/pages-router-audit-checklist.md`.

- **Theme standardisation**: After building section components, extract all hardcoded hex colors, border-radius values, and spacing into a single `src/theme.ts` file:
```typescript
export const theme = {
  colors: { primary: '#1B2A4A', accent: '#C9A96E', bg: '#F5F5F0', text: '#444', muted: '#666', border: '#e0e0e0' },
  radii: { sm: '8px', md: '12px', lg: '16px', full: '50px' },
  shadows: { card: '0 4px 20px rgba(0,0,0,0.08)' },
  fonts: { body: "'Inter', sans-serif", heading: "'Playfair Display', serif" },
  spacing: { section: '4rem 1rem', card: '1.5rem', btn: '0.85rem 2.5rem' },
  sizes: { maxWidth: '1200px', contentWidth: '800px' },
}
```
Import in components: `import { theme } from '../theme'` then `style={{ background: theme.colors.primary }}`. Destructure frequently-used keys at top of file: `const c = theme.colors, r = theme.radii, s = theme.spacing`.

- **Mobile navigation**: The hamburger button in Header.tsx is often hidden (`display: none`) in the initial scaffold. Enable it with a `@media (max-width: 768px)` CSS-in-JS block that toggles nav visibility via React state:
```tsx
<style jsx>{`
  @media (max-width: 768px) {
    button { display: block !important; }
    nav { display: ${open ? 'flex' : 'none'} !important; flex-direction: column; ... }
  }
`}</style>
```

- **Footer social links**: site.json typically has `social: { linkedin, instagram }` but the Footer component doesn't render them. Add `socialLinks` to the footer content object and render as `<a>` tags with the site.json social URLs.

- **Cache headers for SSR pages**: `getServerSideProps` reads JSON from disk on every request. Add caching via `res.setHeader('Cache-Control', 'public, s-maxage=60, stale-while-revalidate=300')` or return a `headers` key from `getServerSideProps` (Next.js 13+ supports returning `headers` from `getServerSideProps`).

- **Navigation hrefs**: The content may use multi-tenant paths like `/s/es/nexa-paraguay/programas`. For standalone deployment, replace with plain paths: `/programas`. Check ALL occurrences in ALL content JSON files (es, en, nl, de) — nav item hrefs, hero CTA links, hero CTA secondary links, blog-related URLs, and ANY href field. A missed multiclient path in content/en.json nav hrefs causes EVERY nav link in the EN locale to 404. The Header component must prepend the locale prefix to nav hrefs (e.g., `/servicios` → `/en/servicios`) after stripping any remaining multiclient prefix. Use a grep across all locale files: `grep -rn '/s/' content/*.json`.
- **Placeholder template variables**: Content may have `{{taxRate}}` placeholders. If the component doesn't replace them, pre-replace in the content JSON with the actual value. Search across ALL pages content, not just home.

- **Blog post links must include locale prefix**: Blog "Leer más" / "Read more" links in the BlogSection component must prepend the current locale. Without this, blog links on EN/NL/DE pages navigate to `/blog/post-slug` (no locale) which hits the catch-all route without the locale prefix, causing a 404 or wrong-content render. Fix: use `useRouter` from `next/router` and prepend: `href={\`/${locale}/blog/${post.slug}\`}`. The locale variable comes from the page component's `locale` prop, NOT from `router.locale` (which may be undefined in Pages Router).
- **Content JSON corruption from patch tool**: The `patch` tool can corrupt large JSON files (208KB, 900+ lines) by misplacing commas. A missing comma between `}` and `\"seo\"` breaks the entire site — every page SSR returns `content: {}` with zero Docker errors. **Always validate after every JSON edit**: `python3 -c \"import json; json.load(open('content/es.json')); print('VALID')\"`. For safety, prefer Python manipulation: `json.load()` + `json.dump()` instead of `patch` or `sed` on large JSON files. Add pre-deploy validation: `for f in content/*.json; do python3 -c \"import json; json.load(open('$f'))\" && echo \"$f: VALID\" || exit 1; done`.
- **Docker service labels at service-level**: In `docker-compose.yml`, Traefik labels must be at `services.web.deploy.labels` level, NOT at `services.web.labels`. Docker Compose standalone ignores `deploy:` labels.
- **Content items use q/a keys**: FAQ, privacy, and glossary items in content JSON may use `q`/`a` keys instead of `pregunta`/`respuesta` or `question`/`answer` or `term`/`definition`. Support ALL variants in component logic: `item.q || item.pregunta || item.question || item.title` and `item.a || item.respuesta || item.answer || item.description || item.body`. Prefer `q`/`a` first since that's the most common format in the content source.
- **Hero CTA hrefs share the same problem**: Nav hrefs aren't the only place multiclient paths hide. Hero CTAs (`home.hero.ctaPrimaryHref`, `home.hero.ctaSecondaryHref`) in EN/NL/DE locale files also use `/s/en/nexa-paraguay/` paths. These cause the hero buttons on non-ES locales to 404. Run a systematic grep: `grep -rn 'cta.*Href' content/*.json | grep '/s/'`.
- **Section components receive resolved data directly**: When dispatching to SECTION_MAP, pass `resolveContent(content, section.content)` as `pageContent`, NOT the full page build result. Otherwise the component needs to dig through intermediary keys. Accept both `pageContent` (direct data) and `data` (backward-compat) in component props.
- **SECTION_MAP must be in both index.tsx and [slug].tsx**: Both files dispatch section components. Always add new entries to both.
- **Rich fallback for [slug].tsx**: The generic section renderer in `[slug].tsx` must handle more than just `headline`/`subheadline`. Add eyebrow text, body content paragraphs, and item grids (for FAQ, glossary, services, blog posts, team members). The renderer should detect `data.items`, `data.groups`, `data.pillars` arrays and render them as card grids. Each item card shows `title`/`pregunta`/`question`/`term` + `description`/`respuesta`/`answer`/`definition`/`body`.
- **buildPageContent removal breaks section rendering**: Removing `buildPageContent` is recommended (it's redundant once the dispatch passes sectionData directly), BUT you must reconstruct `pageContent` with the correct key name. Section components in `sections.tsx` access data via `pageContent.hero` — they expect the content key's LAST segment (`home.hero` → `hero`), NOT the section id (`hero` happens to match but `stats-counter` doesn't match `stats`). The dispatch must use `section.content?.split('.').pop()` as the pageContent key. Without this, ALL section data appears invisible despite the component rendering — the hero background image won't load, trust/programs/process images won't appear, and stats won't show values. See the "Passing Images to Section Components" section above for the correct dispatch code.
- **Every page config needs SLUG_MAP entry**: If a new page config JSON is added to `nexa-pages/`, it MUST also be added to `SLUG_MAP` in `[slug].tsx` or it will 404. The `SLUG_MAP` is the routing layer between URL slugs and page config filenames.
- **image paths in images.json**: After copying `images.json` from the paragu-ai-builder source, ALL paths use `/sites/nexa-paraguay/images/...` prefix which only works in the multi-tenant context. For standalone deployment, batch-replace to `/images/...`. Use a Python script to walk the JSON and fix all `src`, `fallbackSrc`, `fallback`, and `basePath` fields.
- **Image path verification**: After fixing paths, verify every referenced image exists on disk with a script. The `images.json` may reference images that don't exist yet. Run `python3 -c "..."` to compare referenced src paths against actual files in `public/images/`.
- **Docker service update may not deploy new config**: After `docker build` and `docker service update --image`, the new container may still have old content JSON due to Docker build cache layers. Always verify inside the container: `docker exec $(docker ps --filter name=<service> -q) cat /app/nexa-pages/home.json | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print([s['id'] for s in d['sections']])"`. If old, force a restart: `docker service update --force <service>`.
- **Content duplication across pages is common**: Pages Router section-driven sites easily accumulate duplicate content when page configs share section IDs or content paths point to identical data. Always run a systematic scan (see `references/content-duplication-audit-pattern.md`) before deploying new pages.

## Partial Pre-Rendering (PPR) with Next.js 16

Next.js 16's PPR (`experimental.ppr: true` + `cacheComponents: true`) streams dynamic content over a static HTML shell. Implemented on Nexa Paraguay.

### Requirements

```js
// next.config.js
const nextConfig = {
  experimental: {
    ppr: true,
    pprCacheComponents: true,  // or: ppr: { cacheComponents: true }
  }
}
```

### Constraints (PPR strictly enforces these)

With `cacheComponents: true`, the following are **forbidden** in page/layout exports:
- `revalidate` (export or segment config)
- `dynamicParams`
- `dynamic`
- `fetch` with `revalidate`/`cache` options
- `generateStaticParams` must be `async` but `generateMetadata` must be **sync**

To bypass: put uncached data inside `<Suspense>` children that use `use` (React 19) or `fetch`. The static shell renders immediately, dynamic content streams in.

```tsx
// [locale]/page.tsx — PPR-ready pattern
export const experimental_ppr = true
// NO revalidate, NO dynamic, NO dynamicParams exports

export async function generateStaticParams() {
  // returns all locale+slug combos for PPR to pre-render
}

// Synchronous generateMetadata required for PPR
export function generateMetadata({ params }: { params: any }) {
  const { content } = getPageData(params.locale)
  return { title: content?.seo?.title, description: content?.seo?.description }
}

export default function Page({ params }: { params: any }) {
  const { content } = getPageData(params.locale)
  return (
    <>
      {/* Static section — renders in HTML shell */}
      <HeroSection data={content.hero} />
      {/* Dynamic section — streams in */}
      <Suspense fallback={<Skeleton />}>
        <DynamicContent />
      </Suspense>
    </>
  )
}
```

### Data Loading Pattern

Since `generateStaticParams` must be async but `generateMetadata` must be sync, load data with a **memoized sync function** that reads from disk or cache:

```tsx
// lib/page-data.ts — sync, reads from filesystem at build time
import { readFileSync, existsSync } from 'fs'
import { join } from 'path'

const LOCALES = ['es', 'en', 'nl', 'de']
const REPO = process.cwd()

function loadPageData(locale: string, slug?: string) {
  // Read content JSON
  const content = JSON.parse(readFileSync(join(REPO, 'content', `${locale}.json`), 'utf-8'))
  // Read page config JSON
  const pageConfig = slug && slug !== 'home'
    ? JSON.parse(readFileSync(join(REPO, 'nexa-pages', `${slug}.json`), 'utf-8'))
    : { sections: [{ id: 'home', content: 'home' }] }
  return { content, pageConfig }
}

export { loadPageData, LOCALES }
```

### Static Shell Content

Components rendered OUTSIDE `<Suspense>` must not use:
- `useRouter()`, `useSearchParams()`, `usePathname()` — these are dynamic
- `new Date()` in JSX — creates dynamic content; hardcode years
- `window`, `localStorage`, `document` — SSR will throw
- `headers()` or `cookies()` in page components

For layout components like footer that need a year, hardcode it or use `export const dynamic = 'force-static'` on a separate component:

```tsx
// Footer with static year — avoids uncached data
const COPYRIGHT_YEAR = '2026'
// NOT: String(new Date().getFullYear())
```

### JSON Data Nesting

Blog posts stored as `{ "posts": [...] }` (object with array, not flat array). The `posts` key is the wrapper — access with:
```tsx
const posts = JSON.parse(readFileSync(path))
const list = posts.posts || posts  // handle both formats
```

### Build Verification

```bash
# Build output shows ◐ for PPR pages, ○ for static, ƒ for dynamic
npx next build | grep -E '(◐|○|ƒ)'

# Expected output:
# ◐ /[locale]           Partial Prerender
# ◐ /[locale]/[slug]    Partial Prerender
# ◐ /[locale]/blog/[slug] Partial Prerender
# ○ /admin              Static
# ƒ /api/contact        Dynamic
```

### Pitfalls

- **PPR + `useRouter` conflict**: Client components inside PPR pages can use `useRouter`, but if they're outside Suspense boundaries, they can break static shell rendering. Either wrap in `<Suspense>` or use `next/dynamic` with `{ ssr: false }`.
- **Skeleton fallbacks must be lightweight**: The fallback prop in `<Suspense>` renders instantly — keep it minimal (single div with spinner or shimmer classes). Heavy skeletons defeat PPR's purpose.
- **`posts.json` format variance**: Some implementations have `{ posts: [...] }`, others have a flat array. The component reading it must handle both. See "JSON Data Nesting" above.
- **Pitfall: Footer `new Date()` blocks PPR**: Even a single `new Date().getFullYear()` call in a server component renders the entire page as dynamic. Replace with a hardcoded year string.

## Multi-Locale Routing (Activation Pattern)

All four locales (`es`, `en`, `nl`, `de`) should be activated as infrastructure, not just the default. The pattern:

### Middleware Locale Detection

**CRITICAL: Cookie name must match getServerSideProps.** The middleware reads `NEXT_LOCALE` cookie, and getServerSideProps sets it as `NEXT_LOCALE`. If they disagree, Accept-Language header overrides on every visit. Also check `request.cookies.get('locale')` for backward compatibility with any previous middleware version.

- **Factory-demo content ships by default**: Fabricated stats (+500 families, 98% success), AI headshots, fake testimonials — these are NOT placeholders the client filled in, they're the scaffold. Replace them before anyone sees the site. Check `is_demo` flag in site.json.
// src/middleware.ts
const LOCALES = ['es', 'en', 'nl', 'de']
const DEFAULT_LOCALE = 'es'

export function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  const { pathname } = request.nextUrl
  const firstSegment = pathname.split('/')[1]
  const hasLocale = LOCALES.includes(firstSegment)
  if (!hasLocale && !pathname.startsWith('/_next') && !pathname.startsWith('/images') && pathname !== '/favicon.svg') {
    // Read NEXT_LOCALE first (set by getServerSideProps), fall back to 'locale' (legacy)
    let preferred = request.cookies.get('NEXT_LOCALE')?.value || request.cookies.get('locale')?.value || DEFAULT_LOCALE
    if (!LOCALES.includes(preferred)) {
      const acceptLang = request.headers.get('accept-language') || ''
      for (const loc of LOCALES) {
        if (acceptLang.startsWith(loc) || acceptLang.includes(` ${loc}`) || acceptLang.includes(`;${loc}`)) {
          preferred = loc; break
        }
      }
    }
    const url = new URL(`/${preferred}${pathname}`, request.url)
    if (request.nextUrl.search) url.search = request.nextUrl.search
    return NextResponse.redirect(url, 302)
  }
  const result = NextResponse.next()
  return result
}

export const config = {
  matcher: ['/((?!_next|images|favicon\\.svg).*)'],
}
```

### Locale Detection in getServerSideProps — URL Prefix MUST Win

**This is the most common multi-locale bug.** The URL prefix (`/es/servicios`) must ALWAYS override cookie and Accept-Language. Without tracking whether the URL explicitly specified a locale, a stale `NEXT_LOCALE=en` cookie will override `/es/`:

```typescript
export function getServerSideProps({ params, req, res }: any) {
  let slug = Array.isArray(params?.slug) ? params.slug.join('/') : (params?.slug || 'home')
  let locale = 'es'
  let hasUrlLocale = false
  const LOCALES = ['es', 'en', 'nl', 'de']

  // 1) URL prefix locale takes highest priority
  if (slug.includes('/')) {
    const parts = slug.split('/')
    if (LOCALES.includes(parts[0])) {
      locale = parts[0]; hasUrlLocale = true
      slug = parts.slice(1).join('/') || 'home'
    }
  } else if (LOCALES.includes(slug)) {
    locale = slug; hasUrlLocale = true; slug = 'home'
  }

  // 2) If no URL locale, check cookie (only if URL didn't specify)
  if (!hasUrlLocale) {
    const cookieLocale = req?.cookies?.NEXT_LOCALE
    if (cookieLocale && LOCALES.includes(cookieLocale)) {
      locale = cookieLocale
    }
  }

  // 3) If still default and no cookie, detect from Accept-Language (only if URL didn't specify)
  const noCookie = !req?.cookies?.NEXT_LOCALE
  if (!hasUrlLocale && locale === 'es' && noCookie) {
    const detected = detectLocaleFromHeader(req?.headers?.['accept-language'])
    if (detected) locale = detected
  }

  // Set locale cookie for persistence
  const cookieMaxAge = 60 * 60 * 24 * 365
  res.setHeader('Set-Cookie', `NEXT_LOCALE=${locale}; Path=/; Max-Age=${cookieMaxAge}; SameSite=Lax`)
  // ...
}
```

### Header: Nav Hrefs Must Prepend Locale Prefix

After stripping any multiclient prefix (`/s/en/nexa-paraguay/`), prepend the current locale to every nav href. The language switcher must also be in the header — implement a dropdown with flag emoji + language code:

```tsx
// In Header.tsx
const LOCALE_FLAGS = { es: '🇪🇸 ES', en: '🇬🇧 EN', nl: '🇳🇱 NL', de: '🇩🇪 DE' }
const currentLocale = locale || 'es'

function switchLocale(newLocale: string) {
  const path = router.asPath
  const LOCALES = ['es', 'en', 'nl', 'de']
  let cleanPath = path
  const parts = path.split('/').filter(Boolean)
  if (parts.length > 0 && LOCALES.includes(parts[0])) {
    cleanPath = '/' + parts.slice(1).join('/') || '/'
  }
  window.location.href = '/' + newLocale + cleanPath
}

// Nav item href rendering
let href = item.href || '#'
// Strip existing multiclient prefix
href = href.replace(/^\/s\/[^/]+\/[^/]+/, '')
// Prepend locale prefix for internal links
if (!href.startsWith('http') && !href.startsWith('/' + currentLocale)) {
  href = '/' + currentLocale + href
}
```

### Locale JSON Key Parity Verification

Write a comparison script (`scripts/compare-locale-keys.py`) that walks key paths across all locale JSONs using dot-separated paths and finds truly missing keys (not structural differences). Add empty strings for missing keys:

```python
import json
def walk(obj, path=''):
    results = set()
    if isinstance(obj, dict):
        for k, v in obj.items():
            p = f'{path}.{k}' if path else k
            results.add(p)
            results |= walk(v, p)
    elif isinstance(obj, list) and obj and isinstance(obj[0], dict):
        for i, item in enumerate(obj):
            results |= walk(item, path)
    else:
        results.add(path)
    return results
```

### Catch-All Route [[...slug]] Instead of [slug]

To support `/en/faq` style multi-segment URLs, use `[[...slug]]` NOT `[slug]`:

```bash
mv src/pages/"[slug].tsx" src/pages/"[[...slug]].tsx"
```

This captures `/` → empty array, `/faq` → `['faq']`, `/en/faq` → `['en', 'faq']`. `index.tsx` CANNOT coexist — delete it and handle the home page case within `[[...slug]]`.

### Locale Extraction

```typescript
export function getServerSideProps({ params }: any) {
  let slug = Array.isArray(params?.slug) ? params.slug.join('/') : (params?.slug || 'home')
  let locale = 'es'
  const LOCALES = ['es', 'en', 'nl', 'de']
  if (slug.includes('/')) {
    const parts = slug.split('/')
    if (LOCALES.includes(parts[0])) { locale = parts[0]; slug = parts.slice(1).join('/') || 'home' }
  }
  const fullContent = loadJSON(process.cwd() + '/content', `${locale}.json`) || ...
}
```

### Locale JSON Key Parity

Before activating, audit all locale JSON files against the reference (`es.json`). Walk every key and check it exists in each locale. Add missing keys with empty strings (placeholders until translated). The gaps are typically 10-15 keys per locale: new pages not propagated, recently-added content keys, object sub-keys.

## Asset Activation Policy

Nothing gets deleted without client confirmation. Every dormant asset is an unactivated feature. Keep all `@ai-whisperers/*` packages installed (tree-shaken at build time, zero runtime cost). Keep unreferenced images on disk and in the manifest (pre-generated for future campaigns, <1MB total).

#### Content JSON Integrity Check (Pre-Deploy)

Before building the Docker image, validate ALL content JSON files. A corrupt JSON makes SSR return `content: {}` (empty pages) with zero errors in Docker logs:

```bash
python3 -c "
import json, os
for f in os.listdir('content'):
    if f.endswith('.json'):
        try:
            json.load(open(f'content/{f}'))
        except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
            print(f'CORRUPT: {f} — {e}')
            exit(1)
print('All content JSON valid')
"
```

### Stale .next/ directory pitfall

After `git pull origin main` that changes content JSON files or source code, the existing `.next/` directory may be stale. The Docker `COPY . .` includes `.next/`, so stale build output + new content = empty SSR pages. Fix:

```bash
rm -rf .next/   # Only when source/content files changed
npm run build    # Rebuild from scratch
```

After any content edit or page config change, validate all section content keys resolve:

```python
import json, os
with open('content/es.json') as f: content = json.load(f)
for fname in os.listdir('nexa-pages'):
    cfg = json.load(open(f'nexa-pages/{fname}'))
    for sec in cfg.get('sections', []):
        ck = sec.get('content', sec['id'])
        resolved = content
        for part in ck.split('.'):
            if isinstance(resolved, dict) and part in resolved: resolved = resolved[part]
            else: resolved = None; break
        if resolved is None:
            print(f"MISSING: {fname} → {sec['id']} → {ck}")
```

## Client Site Deep-Dive — Full Business Assessment

When a client site already exists with docs (like Nexa Paraguay), don't just look at code — produce a structured assessment that covers ALL areas the business owner needs to see.

### Assessment Structure

1. **Code & Content Audit** — browse the live site + examine repo structure + read all docs
2. **Competitive Position** — price positioning, AI readiness, niche focus (use existing docs if they exist)
3. **Financial Model Review** — unit economics, funnel estimates, cost structure (which docs exist)
4. **Gap Analysis** — what's broken now (P0/P1) vs what needs client input
5. **Phased Roadmap** — organize into 6 phases by impact: (1) Fix broken pages, (2) Conversion path, (3) Trust & credibility, (4) SEO & analytics, (5) Multi-language, (6) AI differentiation

### Quick-Win Batch Sizing

Always include a "quick-wins I can execute right now" section with concrete, batch-fixable items:
- Fix page config `id` fields across all page JSONs
- Add sitemap.xml + robots.txt
- Replace fabricated stats with honest text
- Fix blog image key mismatches
- Remove `is_demo` flag
- Add unique meta titles per page
- Enable additional locale routing

### Business Model Impact Table

Show the before/after for the client in financial terms they understand:

| Metric | Current | With improvements |
|---|---|---|
| Clients/month | X | Y |
| Avg ticket | $Z | $W |
| Monthly revenue | $A | $B |

### Key Questions to Answer

- What's the AI differentiation opportunity? (Check competitor landscape — most have zero AI)
- What justifies the premium pricing? (AI layer, multilingual, faster process)
- What's the head start window? (Usually 12-18 months before competitors catch up)
- What are the 3-5 blog posts that would dominate zero-competition keywords?
